title: Cyberreality on Composing in the Dark
subtitle: The Mexico City producer talks process, patience, and building sonic worlds from silence
author: AXIS Editorial
excerpt: In a basement studio tucked beneath Colonia Roma, Cyberreality builds the kind of music that takes time to understand — and longer to forget.

content.text: It is difficult to locate Cyberreality within any single genre. His sets move through industrial textures, ambient drift, and oblique dance structures without ever arriving at a conclusion. You get the feeling that he knows exactly where the music is going — he simply refuses to take you there directly.

We met in his studio — three monitors, two synthesizers, and a wall of patch cables arranged with the precision of someone who thinks spatially about sound. He made coffee before we started, and offered no explanation for the hour-long drone piece playing quietly in the background.
On the Process

'I don't write tracks,' he says, correcting himself immediately. 'I make spaces. The music is just what happens when you move through them.' He gestures to the rack beside him, a stack of modules whose names mean nothing to me. 'This one creates a kind of friction. A resistance. And when you push against it, it pushes back. That's when it gets interesting.'
The best music is the kind that knows something you don't. You keep listening because you think eventually it'll tell you what.

He played at AXIS Episode I last December, a set that ran long and nobody minded. The crowd — artists, developers, curators — stood in the dark and listened the way people listen to things they don't entirely understand but trust.
On AXIS

When I ask what drew him to the AXIS context specifically, he is quiet for a moment. 'The audience knows what they came for. They're not there by accident. There's no background noise of people who wandered in off the street.' He pauses. 'Which means if you lose them, you really lost them. It's honest.'

Before I leave, he puts on something new. It hasn't been released. It might not be. It sounds like a building deciding whether to collapse. I ask him what it's called. He says it doesn't have a name yet. 'You name things when you're done with them,' he says. 'I'm not done.'

title: AXIS 4.0 Confirmed for Mexico City, March 21
subtitle: The fourth edition marks the most ambitious program in the series history
author: AXIS Editorial
excerpt: AXIS 4.0 takes over an undisclosed industrial space in Mexico City on March 21, bringing together artists, developers, and communities across four hours of live programming.

content.text: AXIS 4.0 is confirmed for March 21, 2026, in an industrial space in Mexico City that will be disclosed to confirmed attendees 48 hours before the event. The fourth edition of the series marks the most expansive program in its brief history, with live performances, interactive installations, live-streamed talks, and an onchain mint available to anyone holding a valid access pass.

The evening will open at 18:00 CST with an hour of ambient programming before the first of two main performances begins. A second stage will run parallel talks and demonstrations, featuring artists working at the intersection of code and spatial experience.
What to Expect

Attendees can expect the usual AXIS characteristics — low lighting, unusual spatial configurations, and a curation that makes no effort to be accessible. What the team describes as 'earned familiarity' — an experience that rewards attention over novelty.
We don't design for first impressions. We design for the third time you think about it.

Access for Episode IV is tiered: open registration through Luma for general attendance, priority access for AXIS membership holders, and reserved positions for allied protocols and partners. The onchain component — a digital artifact unique to this edition — will be mintable by all verified attendees via the AXIS app on Base.
Doors open at 17:30. The space will be revealed via push notification to the AXIS app at 18:00 on March 19. Register now through Luma to secure your spot before capacity closes.

title: Spaces That Were Never Meant for This
subtitle: On the politics of choosing the wrong room
author: R. Montoya
excerpt: There is something that happens when art enters a space that was designed for something else entirely. The awkwardness is not a problem. It is the point.

content.text: The white cube gallery is one of the most thoroughly theorized spaces in contemporary art. It is also one of the most exhausted. Its neutrality — once radical — has become a kind of silence that no longer generates meaning. We are left with rooms that say nothing because they have been designed to say nothing, on the assumption that art will fill the gap.

What happens when you remove this assumption? What happens when art enters a space that was designed for something else entirely — a factory floor, a parking structure, a transit terminal, a basement without a clear purpose? The answer is: something useful. Not always beautiful. Often uncomfortable. But useful.
Resistance as Condition

The resistance of a non-gallery space is not an obstacle. It is a condition. The dripping pipe, the industrial smell, the bad acoustics, the wrong proportions — these create friction. Friction is what forces both artist and audience to recalibrate. You cannot coast on the neutrality of a white wall when there are no white walls.
To exhibit in a wrong space is to admit that rightness is never neutral. It is always someone's decision, someone's enforcement.

AXIS understood this from the beginning. Its locations — announced hours before events begin, revealed to those who are already committed to showing up — perform a kind of spatial trust. You go without knowing where you're going. You arrive at something that was not designed for your arrival. This is, of course, the condition of most meaningful experience.

The site-specific work that has moved me most in recent years has been work that failed to be comfortable in its space. Failed productively. Failed in ways that made visible what the space was actually for, what it was built to serve, who it excluded. Art that fits its room too well is decoration. Art that refuses to fit is conversation.
The Space Speaks

The question for artists working in non-traditional spaces is not how to overcome the space's limitations. It is how to use them. How to let the building speak through the work. How to let the space's history — industrial, municipal, commercial, abandoned — enter the meaning of what is being shown. The walls have already been saying something. The art just needs to listen first.

title: The New Underground
subtitle: How Mexico City's experimental scene survived its own success
author: D. Varela
excerpt: Five years ago, calling something 'underground' in Mexico City meant something specific. Now it means something else. Neither meaning is wrong.

content.text: The word 'underground' has undergone the usual pressures. It began as a descriptor — music and spaces operating outside established commercial and institutional circuits. Then it became a marketing category. Then a lifestyle identifier. Now it exists in some recursive state where it refers to itself and to its own contamination simultaneously.

In Mexico City, this process has been accelerated by external attention. As the city became globally legible — Spotify editorial, New York Times real estate sections, the predictable apparatus of cultural tourism — its scenes acquired a new problem: they became interesting to people for whom being interesting is a primary value.
What Remains

And yet. Something persists in the basement shows, the rooftop parties, the warehouses in Neza and Iztapalapa that no travel writer will ever reach, the afternoon events in spaces that don't have Instagram accounts because the people who run them don't have Instagram accounts. Something that operates by different logic — not anti-commercial exactly, but not oriented toward commerce either.
The underground didn't disappear when it became visible. It moved. It always moves. The question is whether you can keep up.

AXIS sits at an interesting point in this geography. It is professionally organized — there are smart contracts, onchain artifacts, livestreams reaching audiences in Seoul and São Paulo. But its events are deliberately hard to find, its curation is not designed for virality, and its community is small enough that you will see the same faces every month.

This is a specific kind of professionalism: one that uses sophisticated tools to stay small. To remain porous. To keep the event legible to the people who were supposed to be there, and slightly opaque to everyone else. In a city of 22 million, that is a precise and difficult thing to maintain.

title: AR and the Dissolution of the Gallery Wall
subtitle: Augmented reality is not a replacement for physical space. It is its shadow.
author: L. Ochoa
excerpt: The most interesting question about AR in art is not how it enhances experience. It is what it reveals about the experience we already thought we were having.

content.text: Augmented reality in art has been promised as a liberation from the physical — a way to extend the gallery into the street, to place artworks in unexpected contexts, to reach audiences who will never enter a formal exhibition space. This is true, and it is also insufficient.

What is more interesting, and less discussed, is what AR reveals about the assumptions we carry into physical spaces. When you point a phone at a blank wall and something appears, the question is not only what appears. The question is: what were you already seeing before the app opened?
The Layer Problem

AR adds a layer. But all experience is already layered. When we enter a gallery, we carry with us the accumulated weight of every gallery we have ever entered. We carry expectations, protocols, trained responses. We know to walk slowly. We know not to touch. We know where the label will be. AR doesn't dissolve these layers — it adds another one on top of them.
The medium is not the message here. The space is the message. The AR just asks you to notice it.

AXIS's AR component — deployed through its custom app on Base — asks attendees to point their phones at designated points in the physical space to unlock layered content: sound, image, animation, text. This is simple. But the simplicity is doing something. It is making visible the act of attention. It is forcing you to look at a specific wall, corner, or surface at a specific moment.

The gallery wall does not disappear under AR. It becomes more itself — a surface on which choices are made, by which attention is organized, through which some things are shown and others hidden. AR is not a liberation from this. It is, at best, an honest acknowledgment of it.

title: Episode III: What Happened in the Warehouse
subtitle: A dispatch from AXIS's third edition in Mexico City
author: AXIS Editorial
excerpt: AXIS Episode III ran four hours over schedule. Nobody complained. This is a report from inside.

content.text: The address came through at 17:48, twelve minutes before the announced start time. By then, most people were already nearby, circling the block in the way you circle something you can't quite identify. The space was a warehouse in Colonia Industrial — 800 square meters of concrete, bad lighting, and a loading dock that had been converted into a secondary stage.

Episode III was the most ambitious program AXIS had attempted at that point. Two live sets, three installations, an AR layer activated by the app, and a panel conversation that started at midnight. The crowd peaked at around 300 and stayed there for most of the night.
The Music

Omarov opened. His set — an hour of layered percussion and sampled speech — established the pace. Not fast, not exactly slow, but relentless. Sunshine Vendetta followed with something closer to ambient techno: structured enough to be legible, diffuse enough to work against the space's industrial hardness.
The best part was watching people decide whether they were listening or dancing. Most of them were doing both, badly, which is how it should be.

Mami Pistola closed the main stage at 2:30. Her set drew from recordings made in transit — airports, terminals, the interior of buses — and reprocessed them into something unmistakably local. The samples sat inside the live synthesis like objects inside water, visible but changed.

The installations ran throughout. One piece — a screen mounted at floor level, showing code executing in real time — stopped working for twenty minutes around 1:00 AM. The crowd gathered anyway. Watching a broken installation try to fix itself is, it turns out, more interesting than watching it work.

title: Onchain Provenance and the Death of the Fake
subtitle: What does authenticity mean when the chain never forgets?
author: A. Reyes
excerpt: The art world has always needed fakes. Not to be deceived by them — to define itself against them. What happens when faking becomes impossible?

content.text: Every major forgery scandal in art history has been, at its core, a story about attention. The Elmyr de Hory fakes, the Han van Meegeren Vermeers, the Knoedler Gallery cases — these were not failures of authentication technology. They were failures of institutional attention. People looked, and saw what they expected to see.

Blockchain provenance changes this in a specific way. It doesn't make looking more careful. It makes looking almost irrelevant for certain questions. The chain knows when the token was minted, by whom, and every subsequent transfer. You cannot fake a mint date. You cannot create the appearance of institutional holding without the reality of institutional holding.
What This Doesn't Solve

But blockchain provenance only begins at the moment of minting. What happened before that moment — who made the work, under what conditions, with what materials and intentions — is outside the chain's knowledge. The oracle problem: the chain is only as trustworthy as the information entered into it.
Immutability is not truth. It is the permanent record of whoever got there first.

This is the more interesting problem. Not whether blockchain provenance can eliminate forgery — in narrow cases, it mostly can — but whether it changes what we mean by authenticity. The chain can tell you that this token was minted at block 1,234,567 by this wallet. It cannot tell you whether that matters.

The art world's relationship to authenticity has always been partly about economic value and partly about something harder to name — a sense of contact with an original act of making. Onchain provenance solves the first problem elegantly. The second it cannot touch. The work still has to be made, by someone, for reasons that the chain will never record.

title: Mami Pistola: Sounds Collected in Transit
subtitle: The producer on field recording, airports, and why she never uses headphones on planes
author: AXIS Editorial
excerpt: Mami Pistola records everything. Every taxi, every terminal, every moment of waiting. 'The world is always making music,' she says. 'I just keep the receipts.'

content.text: The phone is always out, always recording. That is the first thing you notice about Mami Pistola — or rather, the first thing she tells you about herself before you notice anything else. 'I record everything,' she says. 'Taxi rides. Waiting rooms. Arguments I'm not involved in. The sound a refrigerator makes at 3 AM.' She pauses. 'Some of it ends up in the music.'

Her set at AXIS Episode III used source material collected across four countries over two years. You couldn't tell — that was the point. The recorded environments became texture, became rhythm, became the grain of the synthesis against which her live processing ran. The transit sounds were there, but they had become something else.
On Field Recording

'People think field recording is romantic,' she says. 'The lone artist with the microphone, capturing the authentic voice of a place. That's a fantasy. What I'm doing is editorial. I record ten hours and use forty seconds. The forty seconds I choose says more about me than about where I was.'
The world is always making music. I just keep the receipts. What I do with the receipts — that's the work.

She plays me three minutes of the source recording from Mexico City's Benito Juárez airport — baggage claim, Terminal 2, a Tuesday afternoon. It is chaotic and somehow rhythmic. There is a recurrence to the announcements. There is a percussion in the bag wheels. I can hear, dimly, what she hears.

'I don't use headphones on planes,' she says. 'Everyone thinks I'm crazy. But the plane is the most interesting sound environment. Three hundred people in a metal tube at 35,000 feet, all pretending they're comfortable. The whole thing vibrates. You can feel it if you pay attention.'
